findings.claim: providers_view (auth_views.py:235-243) refreshes near-expiry tokens whenever the settings/onboarding page polls /api/auth/providers/. django-oauth-toolkit's refresh grant revokes the prior access token server-side (standard DOT behavior, not verified against each provider's deployed version), so a user opening Settings during a materialization running on a near-expiry token would invalidate the token frozen in the run's session headers, killing the run minutes before its TTL. Narrow window (only inside the 5-minute refresh buffer) but applies to all three providers, including Connect's 2-week tokens in their final 5 minutes. Lowest-confidence item in the credential-lifetime cluster (hypothesis: depends on unverified upstream DOT revocation behavior).

findings.title: Retry/error-shape hardening applied only to Connect loaders; OCS/CommCare swallow malformed responses and have no retry
findings.claim: connect_base has bounded urllib3 Retry (backoff, Retry-After, status forcelist) and raises ConnectExportError on missing 'results'/invalid JSON; ocs_base and commcare_base call raise_for_status with no retry, so one transient 5xx fails an entire (non-resumable) source and rolls back everything loaded — OCS messages' N+1 per-session GETs compound exposure and a single deleted-session 404 aborts the whole source. ocs_base._paginate and commcare_cases treat a missing 'results'/'cases' key as an empty page, yielding a source marked completed with 0 rows (silent-empty on the exact envelope-change class that bit Connect before validation was added); _normalize_case defaults every field to ''. CommCare cases also missed the relative-next-URL fix (8774864) applied to forms/metadata, so a non-absolute next URL raises MissingSchema and fails cases.

findings.title: Mid-rematerialization reads: Connect serves silently-partial tables while CommCare/OCS block queries until 30s timeout
findings.claim: The tenant schema stays ACTIVE during re-materialization and context serves it. Connect writers commit DROP+CREATE immediately then grow tables page by page, so concurrent agent queries return empty/partial tables with no warning; CommCare/OCS writers hold DROP inside one transaction, so concurrent queries block on ACCESS EXCLUSIVE until the 30s statement_timeout for the duration of an hours-long load. Two inconsistent, uncommunicated failure modes for the same situation.

issues.title: MCP server hardening: caller auth, connection hygiene, pooling
issues.summary: No caller authentication: tenant-scoped tools trust workspace_id blindly. Add shared-secret auth + membership checks; add dead-DB-connection hygiene (the 22-hour-outage class, fixed only for the worker); pool the managed-DB connection; delete the dead OAuth-token plumbing paid for every chat turn.

findings.title: MCP HTTP server has no caller authentication; teardown_schema/query trust workspace_id blindly; isolation is network topology only
findings.claim: The streamable-HTTP MCP server configures only DNS-rebinding Host checks — no token/secret/principal; auth.py only extracts OAuth tokens. Every tenant-scoped tool (list_tables/describe_table/get_metadata/query/get_lineage/get_schema_status/teardown_schema) resolves context purely from the workspace_id argument with no entitlement check; only run_materialization verifies membership, and even there run_materialization's guard passes for any user when user_id=''. Prod binds 0.0.0.0:8100 on the shared docker network with no proxy, so any co-located process, SSRF, dev port-forward or compromised container can call teardown_schema(confirm=True, workspace_id=<any>) to destroy data or query any schema. cancel_materialization/get_materialization_status accept unscoped LLM-supplied run_ids. Inside chat the args are injected server-side, so exposure depends entirely on deployment network isolation, documented only in a comment.

findings.title: MCP server process has no Django dead-DB-connection hygiene; ORM on asyncio.to_thread pool threads in refresh/view-schema builds also unguarded
findings.claim: The 2026-06-09 22h-outage mechanism (long-lived Django process reuses a dead connection forever after an RDS maintenance window) was fixed only for procrastinate tasks via the TEMPORARY decorator. The MCP server is an identical long-lived Django-ORM process (django.setup, per-tool-call ORM atouch/ThreadJob writes) with zero close_old_connections, no CONN_MAX_AGE/CONN_HEALTH_CHECKS, so after the next RDS restart every ORM-touching MCP tool call fails until process restart — the agent loses query/list_tables/run_materialization wholesale. The decorator's cleanup also only reaches the async-ORM sensitive thread; build_view_schema (heavy sync ORM) runs via asyncio.to_thread at two sites and refresh_tenant_schema runs the entire ORM-writing run_pipeline via to_thread, all on default-executor pool threads with no guard — a pool thread holding a connection that died poisons view-schema rebuilds (surfacing as 'system-side fix required') and refreshes, and the FAILED-persist save() on the same dead connection also raises, leaving the row stuck PROVISIONING.

findings.title: Connection-per-operation cost: fresh TLS psycopg connection per MCP query/describe/list and per artifact query; fresh MCP client + tools/list HTTP round trip per chat request
findings.claim: _execute_async and _execute_async_parameterized open a fresh psycopg TLS connection (sslmode require) per invocation with no pool anywhere in mcp_server, paid by every agent query/describe_table, every list_tables reconciliation, every per-table loop in get_metadata and system-prompt building, and every artifact source query (one agent turn against a 15-table schema can open 20+ serial TLS connections); artifact query-data does the same from the API process. get_mcp_tools constructs a new MultiServerMCPClient and performs a tools/list HTTP round trip on every chat message and resume though tool schemas are static, and _persist_synthetic_failure_message builds the entire agent graph (MCP call, knowledge retrieval, schema fetch, LLM bind, compile) just to append one fixed AIMessage, coupling janitor failure-messaging to MCP availability. No PgBouncer in deploy configs.

findings.title: OAuth-token plumbing into MCP is dead end-to-end with false docstrings; cost paid every chat turn and resume
findings.claim: get_user_oauth_tokens is fetched in chat_view and the resume task and passed to build_agent_graph whose oauth_tokens param is referenced only in the signature/docstring and never read; config['oauth_tokens'] is set top-level (not under configurable, so LangGraph neither injects into tool _meta nor persists it) and read by nothing; extract_oauth_tokens has zero non-test callers and its docstring falsely claims transport-layer injection; TenantContext.oauth_tokens is never populated or read. get_user_oauth_tokens also filters to COMMCARE_PROVIDERS and excludes OCS, which reads like a bug but is inert since the whole transport is dead. Real credentials flow worker-side via aresolve_credential/TenantConnection. ~5 files of misleading vestige actively maintained through the fix chain; tokens may be persisted into LangGraph checkpoint rows for no benefit.

issues.title: Cost/latency floor: prompt caching, history + knowledge budgets, polling
issues.summary: No Anthropic prompt caching anywhere; prune_messages is dead (history replayed unbounded, ~quadratic lifetime cost + eventual context overflow); knowledge injected with no budget; serial per-table TLS connections per cache miss; always-on polling; in-memory knowledge pagination; per-version artifact copies; OCS page_size; uncached me_view re-hitting all providers; DD N+1s.

findings.title: System-prompt assembly opens serial per-table fresh TLS DB connections every 60s cache miss; no Anthropic prompt caching; per-process caches under 4 workers
findings.claim: _build_system_prompt caches per (workspace,user,prompt-hash) with a 60s TTL in a per-process dict; on miss _fetch_schema_context runs ~4 ORM queries plus one pipeline_describe_table per table SERIALLY, each opening a fresh psycopg TLS connection to the managed DB (no pool in mcp_server), plus an atouch() write — up to 4 x U x (T+4) serial round trips/min/workspace on the latency path before the first token. ChatAnthropic is built with only model+max_tokens and no cache_control breakpoint anywhere, so the large static system prompt + ~15 tool schemas are re-billed at full input rate on every LLM call (K+1 calls per K-tool turn), even though the prefix is engineered stable for 60s and is cacheable. Embedding row counts/timestamps in the prompt also changes prompt text across materializations, defeating Anthropic prompt-cache hits. uvicorn --workers 4 means 4 independent caches/circuit-breakers and up to 4x20 checkpointer pool connections.

findings.title: prune_messages is dead code: conversation history replayed unbounded every LLM call, no trimming/summarization/tool-result compaction
findings.claim: prune_messages/DEFAULT_MAX_MESSAGES in agents/graph/state.py have zero callers and the graph applies no cap (recursion_limit caps tool iterations, not history); the docstring advertises a window strategy that does not run. agent_node sends the full message list every call, and query tool results (up to 500 rows of JSON) enter history verbatim and are re-sent on every later LLM call. Per-turn input tokens grow linearly with thread age, lifetime cost ~quadratically, and long threads eventually overflow the context window and fail with a generic error. Compounds the no-prompt-caching cost.

findings.title: Knowledge context injected into every system prompt with no size cap; retriever ignores its relevance argument
findings.claim: KnowledgeRetriever.retrieve(user_question) ignores its argument (never read; sole caller passes nothing) and concatenates ALL KnowledgeEntry rows (full content) and ALL TableKnowledge rows into the system prompt with no budget (only learnings capped at 20), unlike the 6000-char schema budget. With a bulk-import endpoint the prompt can grow arbitrarily and is re-billed on every LLM call. applies_to_tables ('for relevance filtering' per the docstring) is display-only; a duplicated nested '## Knowledge Base' heading appears in every prompt; no zip-bomb/decompressed-size guard on import lets a READ member inflate the section. _build_system_prompt additionally runs pipeline_describe_table per table on every 60s cache miss.

findings.title: OCS loaders use upstream default page_size=100 — ~10-15x more list requests than necessary; ocs_participants docstring says 'no count today' but upstream now provides first-page count
findings.chain: OCS settings DEFAULT_PAGINATION PAGE_SIZE=100, max 1500 -> ocs_sessions/participants send no page_size -> ~10-15x list requests; upstream pagination.py now adds first-page count -> ocs_base._paginate picks it up but ocs_participants:49-55 discards page_total (stale docstring)
findings.claim: Verified: OCS settings set DEFAULT_PAGINATION_CLASS=apps.api.pagination.CursorPagination with PAGE_SIZE=100; max_page_size is 1500 and page_size is a supported query param. Scout's ocs_sessions/ocs_participants send no page_size, so list walks cost ~10-15x the HTTP requests of the loaders' Connect/CommCare mental model (1000/page). One param cuts OCS list traffic an order of magnitude (the messages N+1 detail fetches dominate regardless and are essential). Separately, upstream apps/api/pagination.py now computes a first-page 'count' (comment literally names Scout as the consumer); ocs_base._paginate already picks it up so sessions progress became determinate with zero Scout changes, but ocs_participants discards page_total (always yields None at :49-55) and its docstring 'no count field, so totals are always None' is now stale.

findings.title: Chat rate limiting uses per-process LocMemCache; ineffective across uvicorn workers and Redis is provisioned but unused
findings.claim: chat_rate_limit and DRF throttles store counters in the Django default cache, configured as LocMemCache (per-process) with an in-code NOTE admitting it won't work across workers; production.py never overrides CACHES and the API runs uvicorn --workers 4, so the 20-req/60s chat budget and the anon/user throttles are effectively multiplied by worker count and reset on deploy. The limiter logic itself is sound (though its docstring claims a single atomic read/write it doesn't have). ElastiCache Redis is provisioned, threaded through deploy env and GH secrets, and paid for with zero code consumers (Celery-era vestige; DEPLOYMENT.md still calls it 'caching and Celery broker').

findings.title: Always-on polling (jobs 3s + health 5s) with no visibility gating; each jobs poll triggers API-side janitor reconciliation (~5 DB queries)
findings.claim: WorkspaceJobsProvider polls /jobs/active/ every 3s on every page for the active workspace and NetworkStatusContext polls /health/ every 5s, regardless of document.visibilityState or whether any job exists; each active-jobs request runs ~5 platform-DB queries and the stale-job reconciliation sweep (with potential reconcile writes) per stale job. Idle floor ~1,700 requests/hour per open tab (~165 queries/sec at 100 tabs), multiplying with tabs and users; the reconcile sweep is also the mechanism that makes the false-fail-resume race near-deterministic.

findings.title: Knowledge list endpoint paginates in memory over all rows; pagination has no UI so items beyond page 1 are unreachable
findings.claim: GET /knowledge/ loads and serializes every KnowledgeEntry and AgentLearning in the workspace on every request, merges and sorts the serialized dicts in Python, then slices the requested page (O(total) work per page view). Backend paginates at DEFAULT_PAGE_SIZE=50 and the store keeps pagination metadata, but no component reads it and KnowledgeList renders no paging controls, so items beyond the first 50 are unreachable except via server-side search, and /knowledge/:id deep links only open the edit form when the item is in the loaded first page.

findings.title: Artifact versioning copies full code per update and soft-delete never frees rows; live artifacts re-execute all source queries serially per open with no caching
findings.claim: Artifact.create_new_version writes a full new row (entire React code + data JSON) per update linked via parent_artifact, and deletion is soft with an undelete endpoint so rows are never physically removed — agents iterating on a dashboard accrete a full-source copy per iteration indefinitely. ArtifactQueryDataView executes each source_query serially, each opening a fresh TLS connection and re-running full sqlglot validation, with no result caching or conditional-request support, so a 5-query dashboard costs 5 sequential connect+validate+execute cycles per viewer per open. (Artifact.create_new_version is also a dead duplicate of the inline version-bump in artifact_tool.)

findings.title: Data dictionary view: per-table TableKnowledge N+1, two fresh managed-DB connections per request, and async_to_sync in a sync DRF view
findings.claim: DataDictionaryView._get_from_pipeline calls _get_annotation inside the per-table loop, issuing one TableKnowledge.objects.get per table (N+1; one filter would do); the same request opens two separate fresh managed-DB connections (_get_all_columns and _live_tables_in_schema via pipeline_list_tables) and runs async_to_sync inside a sync DRF view (new event loop per request).

findings.title: me_view recomputes onboarding uncached on every /me poll, re-hitting all three provider APIs and flapping onboarding_complete for token-but-no-tenant users
findings.claim: me_view recomputes onboarding from the DB and, when incomplete, eagerly resolves all three providers with no cache guard (unlike tenant_list_view's 1h TTL). _atry_resolve_provider returns True whenever a token exists and resolve_fn doesn't raise even if it resolved zero tenants (resolve_commcare_domains returns [] without raising), so the SPA's /me poll issues up to 3 live provider API calls every poll for a token-bearing user with zero persisted memberships, and onboarding_complete is reported True transiently while the persisted state stays incomplete — the flag flaps and never stabilizes, re-resolving each poll.

issues.title: Background-work robustness: concurrency, janitors, per-tenant locking
issues.summary: One worker at concurrency 1 serializes all background work; no janitor owns MaterializationRun after worker death (zombie 'doing' jobs); no per-tenant mutual exclusion; ThreadJob races its own dispatch; cancel semantics diverge; FutureApp current_app siblings; TTL rewind; checkpointer pool race; resume vs live turn unserialized; purge orphans; the dependency graph has no owner; stream 300s timeout only checked between events; job/checkpoint tables unpruned.

findings.title: Single worker process at concurrency 1: all background work serialized platform-wide; worker deploy on every merge kills in-flight jobs
findings.claim: deploy-worker.yml runs bare 'procrastinate worker' with no --concurrency (default 1) and no queue, so all materializations, teardown/rebuild tasks, thread resumes, and both periodic janitors serialize through one slot — a long materialization blocks the TTL janitor and ThreadJob janitor entirely and delays every other workspace's work by its full duration (and inflates the created_at-anchored staleness that feeds the false-fail-resume race). tasks.py:368-370 relies on one-task-at-a-time for a timing argument, so the concurrency=1 assumption is load-bearing and unpinned. Every push to main redeploys the worker via Kamal with no stop_wait_time and procrastinate's shutdown_graceful_timeout defaults to None, so a running materialization is SIGKILLed after docker's grace — the deploy-triggered re-arm of the zombie-doing incident class.

findings.title: No janitor reconciles MaterializationRun rows stuck in ACTIVE states after hard worker death; procrastinate stalled-job rescue never wired
findings.claim: A worker that dies hard (SIGKILL after deploy stop-timeout, OOM, host crash) leaves the procrastinate job row 'doing' forever and the MaterializationRun in DISCOVERING/LOADING/TRANSFORMING forever: reconcile_stale_thread_job returns None for status in {todo,doing} (correct per-tick, permanent for zombies) and no code calls procrastinate 3.8's get_stalled_jobs/heartbeats/retry_job (grep: zero 'stalled' hits) and prune_stalled_workers never requeues jobs. No janitor iterates MaterializationRun rows at all. UI shows a frozen progress banner/spinner indefinitely (only escape is manual Stop then Retry); zombie rows block resume cursors from prior PARTIAL runs and are miscounted as active by the cancel endpoint. deploy-worker.yml has no stop_wait/shutdown-graceful-timeout and materializations routinely exceed docker's grace period, so every worker deploy mid-materialization re-arms this — the still-open 2026-05-30 zombie-doing incident class; June-10 fixed the dead-CONNECTION trigger, not the dead-PROCESS one. The docker-compose stack ships no worker at all (CLAUDE.md advertises 'all services'), so every materialization on the self-host compose path hangs forever.

findings.title: No per-tenant mutual exclusion for materialization: thread-scoped guard permits concurrent DROP/CREATE/INSERT on a shared tenant schema
findings.claim: The in-flight dispatch guard filters ThreadJob by thread_id only; an in-code comment admits the materializer has no advisory lock per tenant_schema, so two threads in one workspace, two workspaces sharing a tenant, or a /materialize/retry/ racing a chat dispatch can run parallel run_pipeline against the same physical tenant schema. Interleaved DROP/CREATE/INSERT and per-page commits from two workers can deadlock or produce mixed table contents with both runs recording COMPLETED; the no-natural-PK resumable Connect tables can duplicate. The dead MATERIALIZING state (separate finding) removes the only single-tenant in-progress signal that might have deterred a second dispatch.

findings.title: Procrastinate job/event tables and LangGraph checkpoints never pruned; queue-table growth couples to janitor correctness
findings.claim: The worker runs without a job-retention flag and no remove_old_jobs/builtin maintenance task is scheduled, so procrastinate_jobs/procrastinate_events grow unboundedly (~144 janitor jobs/day plus every materialization/teardown/rebuild/resume). Conversely _procrastinate_job_status returns None for unknown ids and None means 'never touch the row', so if an operator ever prunes the table any still-active ThreadJob referencing a pruned id becomes permanently unreconcilable — retention and reconciliation are coupled but undesigned. The reconciler's status allowlist over raw strings also lets unknown/future procrastinate statuses fall into the act branch, and the dependency is pinned lower-bound-only.

findings.title: ThreadJob created after defer_async (dispatch/creation race); resume lifecycle is a long fix-chain held together by hedge + janitor
findings.chain: mcp_server/server.py:606-635 defer_async then ThreadJob.acreate -> apps/workspaces/tasks.py:363-396 hedge + janitor fallback; TODO at tasks.py:373; duplicated at apps/workspaces/api/materialization_views.py:184-210
findings.claim: run_materialization (and the retry endpoint, duplicating the sequence) defers the procrastinate job before creating the ThreadJob with no shared transaction; the worker hedges with a bounded ~3.75s sleep-poll (_defer_resume_for_job) and otherwise leans on the 15-min janitor, giving a late-visible ThreadJob a phantom spinner up to ~10-25 min. tasks.py:373 TODO documents the clean fix (pre-create with nullable procrastinate_job_id; migration explicitly skipped). The resume/janitor lifecycle is the repo's largest fix-chain (~15-19 commits); invariants live only as CAS filter arguments and scattered comments rather than one owned module. If ThreadJob.acreate AND the rollback abort both fail (both swallowed), the materialization runs fully orphaned — expire_stale_thread_jobs iterates ThreadJobs only, so nothing reconciles it.

findings.title: Two/three materialization-cancel mechanisms with diverging semantics; MCP cancel_materialization writes FAILED not CANCELLED and never stops the load
findings.chain: agent cancel_materialization(run_id) -> mcp_server/server.py:479 run.state=FAILED -> apps/workspaces/tasks.py:485-494 updater raises only on CANCELLED -> load continues -> materializer.py:435-443 CAS miss overwrites result, run FAILED with committed data -> metadata.py:57-67 hides it; contrast apps/workspaces/api/materialization_views.py:22-121 (CANCELLED + abort)
findings.claim: The API cancel view writes CANCELLED + procrastinate abort + ThreadJob flip; the worker's per-page cancellation checkpoint raises only on state==CANCELLED. The MCP cancel_materialization tool instead sets state=FAILED with result.cancelled=True, never aborts the procrastinate job and never touches the ThreadJob, so a mid-LOAD cancel does NOT stop loading — every page/source completes at full provider-API cost; the LOADING->TRANSFORMING CAS then misses and overwrites result, the run ends terminal FAILED with all sources committed, and downstream aggregation reports 'failed' not 'cancelled' (wrong resume message). Catalog only surfaces COMPLETED/PARTIAL runs, so the fully-loaded data presents as 'no data'. cancel_materialization and get_materialization_status also take an unscoped LLM-supplied run_id with no workspace/user binding (info disclosure: status leaks tenant external_id + physical schema name; cancel any in-flight run); HTTP equivalents scope to thread__user. Reviewers disagree whether these tools are in MCP_TOOL_NAMES/bound to the LLM; practical reachability is limited because no tool returns run_id post fire-and-ack.

findings.title: Cross-workspace cancellation: materialization_cancel_view selects orphan runs by shared tenant, cancels the same user's run in a sibling workspace
findings.claim: materialization_cancel_view selects active runs by tenant (tenant schemas are shared across workspaces) and matches ThreadJobs by thread__user only with no workspace filter, so cancelling in workspace A cancels the same user's chat-driven run started from workspace B sharing a tenant and injects a cancelled-resume into B's thread; the orphan path can cancel /refresh/-spawned runs of sibling workspaces. (Tracked jobs are correctly restricted to thread__user.)

findings.title: FutureApp import-time current_app binding (cause of the fixed janitor bug) survives at three sibling sites with swallowed exceptions
findings.claim: tasks.py documents and fixed (28b6647, ORM-based) the bug where module-level `from procrastinate.contrib.django.procrastinate_app import current_app` binds the unresolved FutureApp Blueprint (no job_manager). The identical import-by-value pattern survives at mcp_server/server.py:32, apps/workspaces/api/materialization_views.py:9, and apps/workspaces/api/jobs_cancel.py:11, all used for best-effort cancel/abort wrapped in try/suppress — they work only because views/MCP import after AppConfig.ready(); an import-order regression would silently disable all procrastinate aborts with no signal. The safe lazy ProxyApp spelling is already used by config/procrastinate.py.

findings.title: run_pipeline completion saves stale provision-time last_accessed_at, rewinding the TTL clock by the run duration
findings.claim: run_pipeline saves the TenantSchema at completion with update_fields=['state','last_accessed_at'] using the in-memory instance whose last_accessed_at was captured at provision time (run start), clobbering any newer touch made during the load and starting the 24h TTL clock H hours in the past for an H-hour pipeline — a smaller re-opening of the 2026-06-10 'janitor drops fresh schema' incident class for long loads plus quiet users.

findings.title: Partial-failure or cancel during multi-tenant rematerialization leaves the workspace's own view schema cascade-dropped but ACTIVE
findings.claim: Per-tenant loads DROP TABLE ... CASCADE the tenant's raw tables, cascade-dropping the CURRENT workspace's namespaced ws_* views, but build_view_schema only runs when all_succeeded, and the sibling-rebuild explicitly excludes the current workspace. When one tenant fails or the user cancels mid-loop, the WorkspaceViewSchema stays ACTIVE missing the reloaded tenant's views; the resume's view_schema_failed check only fires when vs.state != ACTIVE, so the agent is told 'partial, answer what you can' / sources 'completed' while every query through the still-ACTIVE view schema hits missing views — the contradictory-catalog condition behind the #190 panic loops, whose canned 're-run materialization' advice happens to be the correct repair. PRs #227-#230 fixed this lie for sibling workspaces and the teardown path but missed the same-workspace partial/cancel path.

findings.title: build_view_schema reactivates EXPIRED view-schema rows without resetting last_accessed_at (incident-b class, view-schema edition)
findings.claim: The 2026-06-10 TTL fix reset last_accessed_at in provision() but not in build_view_schema, which get_or_creates the WorkspaceViewSchema row regardless of state and on success saves only update_fields=['state','last_error'] — a row resurrected from EXPIRED keeps its >24h-old last_accessed_at; teardown_view_schema_task also leaves EXPIRED rows with old timestamps and touch_workspace_schemas only touches ACTIVE/MATERIALIZING rows, so nothing refreshes the timestamp before/during rebuild. expire_inactive_schemas then flips the freshly rebuilt view schema to TEARDOWN on its next */30 tick (window narrowed by atouch on each MCP call). Bonus: the comment claiming build_view_schema saves state=FAILED before re-raising is false for early ValueErrors raised before the row is fetched — rows can park in PROVISIONING with no last_error.

findings.title: Checkpointer pool singleton: unsynchronized init race; force_new closes the shared pool under concurrent streams; no borrow-time health check
findings.claim: apps/chat/checkpointer.py builds a module-global AsyncConnectionPool + AsyncPostgresSaver with no lock: two concurrent cold-start requests interleave (the second closes the first's half-open pool, failing its setup); chat_view retries ANY build_agent_graph exception with ensure_checkpointer(force_new=True), which closes the pool that all other in-flight chat streams in that uvicorn worker are using for checkpoint writes — one request's transient error degrades every concurrent conversation; the pool is created without check= so dead pooled connections are handed out after a DB blip. AsyncPostgresSaver.setup() also runs at first use in every process — LangGraph checkpoint DDL applied at runtime outside Django migration discipline. With 4 workers x max_size 20, checkpointing alone can hold up to 80 platform-DB connections beside ORM and the worker, against db.t4g.micro's ~110 cap.

findings.title: No serialization between a resume ainvoke and a live user chat turn on the same LangGraph thread
findings.claim: chat_view does not check for an active/RUNNING ThreadJob before streaming and the resume task does not check for an in-flight user turn; both invoke the graph against the same thread_id checkpoint concurrently. Dangling-tool-call repairs fix the Anthropic protocol symptom, but interleaved checkpoint writes from two concurrent writers can still interleave or drop superstep state. No CAS exists at this surface, unlike every other multi-writer row on the seam.

findings.title: purge_synced_data orphans WorkspaceViewSchema rows and ws_* physical schemas
findings.claim: The purge_synced_data command tears down all tenant schemas and deletes TenantSchema rows but never touches WorkspaceViewSchema: physical ws_* schemas survive (emptied of views by CASCADE) and their rows keep their prior state (typically ACTIVE) over hollow schemas. Dev-targeted operator command.

findings.title: Dependency graph between tenant schemas and view schemas has no owner: reconciled by 6+ hand-maintained hooks, four live mutation paths lack required hooks
findings.claim: The platform-side dependency graph (WorkspaceTenant) and physical-side graph (pg_depend via DROP CASCADE) are reconciled by at least six hand-maintained hooks scattered across materialize_workspace, teardown_schema, workspace_service, the resume task, chat touch, and MCP context; four live DDL-mutating paths lack required hooks (refresh, MCP teardown tool, transformations trigger, purge command) and the TTL keep-alive clause has five inconsistent implementations (only chat's touch_workspace_schemas is complete; MCP/recipes/artifacts/transformations under-touch, so a multi-tenant workspace kept alive only via non-chat surfaces expires its constituent schemas and the view schema flips FAILED while in active use). Each incident fix (#227-#230) added another hook at another site instead of a choke point — the contract is enforced by convention, already violated four times. When only the view schema expires (tenant data intact), the agent's only recovery is a full run_materialization re-sync because no tool/endpoint exposes the cheap rebuild_workspace_view_schema.

findings.title: Interactive chat stream 300s timeout only checked between events: a stalled LLM/tool call hangs the SSE response and leaks the abandoned generator
findings.claim: langgraph_to_ui_stream computes a deadline but awaits event_stream.__anext__() with no timeout, evaluating the deadline only after an event arrives; a single LLM/tool call that stalls without emitting events makes the TimeoutError branch unreachable, so the 5-minute cap can be greatly exceeded and the response hangs until client disconnect (or a downstream SDK timeout). The resume path bounds the identical ainvoke with asyncio.wait_for; the interactive path did not (fixed-where-it-bit). On the timeout/exception branches the underlying async generator is abandoned without aclose(), so the in-flight ainvoke and its Anthropic call may keep running/billing until GC.

issues.title: Truthful failure: stop rendering errors as success
issues.summary: SSE errors become text with finishReason 'stop'; checkpointer/thread-list outages return []-with-200; cascade-FAILED rows report a fabricated cause + wrong recovery advice; login-resolution failures swallowed (user lands with no data, no error); panic-loop escalation never streamed live.

findings.title: Chat stream protocol swallows errors: timeouts/exceptions become text deltas with finishReason 'stop' — failed runs indistinguishable from success
findings.claim: On TimeoutError or any exception the SSE stream emits an apology as message text and then normal finish markers with finishReason 'stop'; no error chunk type is ever emitted, so useChat's error state only fires for transport failures, a failed agent run is indistinguishable from success to the frontend, and downstream effects (thread-list refresh) treat it as success. Only one endpoint mints a hashed user-visible error_ref; the streaming path returns a bare generic message with no ref, and no request-id/correlation-id propagates across API->MCP->worker. SSE flavor of the 'failures swallowed, caller told completed' pattern from the 2026-06-10 incident.

findings.title: Outage renders as empty-but-successful UI: checkpointer/thread-list load failures return [] with HTTP 200
findings.claim: _load_thread_messages returns [] on any checkpointer error with a 200 response, and the frontend fetchThreads catch sets {threads: [], threadsStatus: 'loaded'}, so during a DB/checkpointer blip users see their conversations apparently deleted with no error state. repair_dangling_tool_calls similarly returns [] on failure (repairs skipped exactly when the checkpointer is flaky). MCP get_schema_status returns the same 'not_provisioned'/empty success envelope for Workspace.DoesNotExist as for a genuinely unprovisioned workspace, inviting materialization against a phantom workspace; a pipeline-registry YAML parse failure silently omits a provider, surfacing as 'No pipeline for provider X' that points at workspace config rather than a broken deploy. Same swallowed-failure family.

findings.title: Cascade-FAILED view schemas report a fabricated cause and wrong recovery advice
findings.claim: _fail_dependent_view_schemas flips sibling WorkspaceViewSchema rows to FAILED without writing last_error, so get_schema_status returns the fallback 'View schema build failed.' — misattributing a tenant-schema teardown cascade as a build failure. The resume prompt built on this status tells the agent 'do NOT re-run materialization; a system-side fix is required', which is wrong advice for this cause (re-materializing the torn-down tenant is the fix). A fresh sibling of the incident-1d 'wrong story told to the agent' class, introduced by the fix wave itself.

findings.title: Login-time tenant resolution failures swallowed: user lands with zero data sources and no error
findings.claim: Post-OAuth tenant resolution for all three providers is wrapped in except Exception: logger.warning, so a provider-API blip at login yields a logged-in user with no TenantMembership rows: no workspace auto-creation, an empty data-sources page, and nothing distinguishing 'resolution failed' from 'account has no opportunities'. The warning is suppressed by Sentry's ERROR event-level default and has no metric filter, so nobody is told. Frontend ensure-tenant failure also only console.errors.

findings.title: Panic-loop escalation message never streamed to live UI; escalation detector couples to FastMCP JSON spacing
findings.claim: escalation_node returns a hardcoded AIMessage rather than calling the LLM, so LangGraph emits no on_chat_model_stream event; langgraph_to_ui_stream only translates on_chat_model_stream + on_tool_end, so the fixed ESCALATION_MESSAGE is never turned into a text-delta and the user sees nothing live after the failing tool cards — it only appears after reload via message_converter. The #190 panic-loop recovery affordance is silently swallowed on exactly the path it was built for. Separately, _should_escalate substring-matches '"code": "NOT_FOUND"' / '"code": "VALIDATION_ERROR"' with a space after the colon, which only matches because FastMCP serializes with indent=2; a serialization change silently disables the circuit breaker, and there is no shared constant across the three layers.

issues.title: Minimum observability: alarms, real health checks, destruction logging
issues.summary: Zero CloudWatch alarms; static health check; no worker/MCP heartbeat; production audit log suppressed (INFO under root WARNING) with empty project_id; DROP SCHEMA CASCADE is silent on success (the 2026-06-10 forensic question is still unanswerable); share tokens/OAuth codes in access logs.

findings.title: No detection layer: zero CloudWatch alarms, static health check, no worker heartbeat; process-death journeys emit no operator signal
findings.claim: infra/scout-stack.yml creates log groups but no CloudWatch alarm/SNS/metric-filter resources; /health/ returns a static 200 with no DB/queue check and exists only on the API container (worker and MCP have no health surface and all three Kamal configs are proxy:false with no healthcheck, so a crash-looping container deploys green). Worker/process death manifests only as the absence of log lines and Task Badger events, which nothing converts to an alert; there is no alarm on jobs-doing-older-than-X, active ThreadJobs older than X, ACTIVE schemas with zero tables, or ACTIVE view schemas with missing physical views. Sentry was live before the 2026-06-09 incident and it still ran ~22h — emitted-but-unrouted signals are the demonstrated failure mode and the dead-process variant has the same blindness today; the exact forensic input (last_accessed_at before overwrite) is also unlogged.

findings.title: Django-side agent audit log dropped in production (INFO under root WARNING) and logs an always-empty project_id; MCP audit has no actor and is logger-only
findings.claim: The only user/thread-attributed record of agent tool calls is the scout.agent.audit logger at INFO, but production LOGGING configures only django/apps/mcp_server loggers with root WARNING, so the line is suppressed entirely in production; it also logs input_state.get('project_id','') which is always empty (state carries workspace_id since the projects->workspaces rename). The MCP-side tool_context audit covers all tools but context_id is workspace_id only (no user/thread), teardown_schema's destructive body has zero logger calls, the trail is a logger-only stderr line into CloudWatch (30-day), and TODO.md's append-only audit DB table is unchecked. Artifact live queries call execute_query directly outside the MCP tool_context wrapper, so they run N stored SQL statements against tenant data with no audit record at all. Langfuse traces only interactive chat; resume turns and recipe runs are dark.

findings.title: Schema destruction is silent on success: TTL janitor and teardown tasks emit zero log lines for DROP SCHEMA CASCADE
findings.claim: expire_inactive_schemas flips schemas ACTIVE->TEARDOWN and defers drops with no logging (no schema id, last_accessed_at, cutoff, or count); teardown_schema/teardown_view_schema_task log only failures, so a successful DROP SCHEMA CASCADE of data-bearing schemas is unlogged and _fail_dependent_view_schemas' count of broken sibling workspaces is discarded. The exact forensic question of the 2026-06-10 incident — why the janitor dropped a fresh schema — remains unanswerable from emitted logs because the decision input (last_accessed_at) is later overwritten. User-facing degradations (OAuth resolution, thread load, panic-loop escalation, stream timeout) are all logged at WARNING that Sentry's ERROR event-level default and the absent metric filters ignore.

findings.title: Share tokens and OAuth codes transit uvicorn/nginx access logs into CloudWatch
findings.claim: Public share endpoints carry the bearer capability in the URL path (/api/chat/threads/shared/<token>/, /api/recipes/runs/shared/<token>/) and OAuth callbacks carry ?code=. Uvicorn's access log is on by default (deploy.yml doesn't disable it; Django LOGGING doesn't govern uvicorn loggers) and frontend nginx logs request lines; both ship to CloudWatch with 30-day retention, so anyone with CloudWatch read access can harvest live share tokens. Not verified against actual prod log output.

issues.title: Close the second auth perimeter (stock allauth /accounts/)
issues.summary: /accounts/ HTML auth is a live second registration perimeter: open self-registration, bypasses the custom rate limiter, login-CSRF/silent linking on GET (SOCIALACCOUNT_LOGIN_ON_GET + AUTO_CONNECT), no working email backend (kills password reset), OCS allowlist open by default.

findings.title: allauth /accounts/ HTML auth surface is a second, ungoverned registration/auth perimeter parallel to the SPA's /api/auth/ paths
findings.chain: config/urls.py:83 include('allauth.urls') -> /accounts/signup/ open form (is_open_for_signup True) creates User+EmailAddress(verified=False, base.py:199); parallel /api/auth/signup/ (auth_views.py:165) creates no EmailAddress; allowlist enforcement only at adapters.py:84-90 (commcare provider only) -> google/github/connect/ocs + both signup forms unrestricted
findings.claim: config/urls.py:83 mounts include('allauth.urls'), exposing the full stock allauth HTML surface in production. (1) /accounts/signup/ is a fully-rendering open self-registration form (DefaultAccountAdapter.is_open_for_signup True, no domain restriction) that creates User + an allauth EmailAddress(verified=False under ACCOUNT_EMAIL_VERIFICATION='optional', base.py:199) — divergent downstream state from /api/auth/signup/ (auth_views.py:165 create_user, which creates ZERO EmailAddress rows). (2) This asymmetry sharpens the known merge-gate finding: signals.py:104 requires EmailAddress(verified=True); /api/auth/signup/ can never satisfy it, and /accounts/signup/ only satisfies it after an email-link click that production cannot deliver. (3) SOCIALACCOUNT_ALLOWED_EMAIL_DOMAINS defaults to {'commcare':['dimagi.com']} (base.py:247) but adapters.py:84-86 returns early for any provider absent from the dict and adapters.py:88-90 for no-email logins — so google/github/commcare_connect/ocs social signup, /accounts/signup/, and /api/auth/signup/ are all unrestricted, with SOCIALACCOUNT_AUTO_SIGNUP=True (base.py:205) auto-creating accounts. The 'allowed email domains' control gates exactly one provider's email and nothing else despite a name implying a registration perimeter. SPA never surfaces /accounts/ but every route is directly reachable.

findings.title: Production has no working email backend: allauth password reset and email verification cannot deliver (also dead-ends the merge gate)
findings.chain: production.py imports base.py; neither sets EMAIL_BACKEND -> Django default smtp localhost:25 -> /accounts/password/reset/ + email verification send_mail -> no MTA in container -> no delivery/ConnectionRefusedError; allauth renders generic success regardless
findings.claim: Neither base.py nor production.py sets EMAIL_BACKEND (only development.py:10 console and test.py:41 locmem do). Under config.settings.production this resolves to Django's default smtp backend at localhost:25 with DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL=webmaster@localhost. The live allauth /accounts/password/reset/ and email-verification flows send via smtp://localhost:25; a Fargate/ECS app container has no local MTA, so sends either 500 (ConnectionRefusedError) or silently never deliver, while allauth still renders a generic 'we sent you an email' success page (anti-enumeration). Local-account password reset is non-functional in production, and this dead-ends the one path (email verification) that could mark an EmailAddress verified and unlock the account-merge gate.

findings.title: allauth HTML auth surface bypasses the custom rate limiter, splits the brute-force budget, and shares per-process LocMemCache
findings.chain: rate_limiting.check_rate_limit/record_attempt only at auth_views.py:109,150; allauth views use separate allauth RATE_LIMITS with different keys; both via django.core.cache -> base.py:318-322 LocMemCache per-process -> Nx under N workers + split budget across two surfaces
findings.claim: apps/users/rate_limiting.py (5/300s per email) is wired ONLY into login_view (auth_views.py:109) and signup_view (auth_views.py:150), not onto any /accounts/ view. allauth 65 does ship active defaults (login 30/m/ip, login_failed 10/m/ip + 5/300s/key, signup 20/m/ip, reset 20/m/ip + 5/m/key) so /accounts/login/ is not fully unthrottled, but: (1) the two surfaces use different cache keys (auth_attempts:<email> vs allauth/rl/...) and never share a budget — a brute force can be split across /api/auth/login/ and /accounts/login/ to double effective attempts; (2) both back onto LocMemCache (base.py:318-322, with the repo's own worker-warning comment at 324-325), so under N uvicorn workers the effective limit is ~Nx and every deploy resets all counters. Extends the known 'Chat rate limiting uses per-process LocMemCache' finding to the auth surface with the extra split-budget dimension.

findings.title: SOCIALACCOUNT_LOGIN_ON_GET=True + EMAIL_AUTHENTICATION_AUTO_CONNECT=True: login-CSRF and silent forced account-linking on GET
findings.chain: base.py:204 LOGIN_ON_GET=True -> /accounts/<provider>/login/ initiates OAuth on GET (no CSRF); allauth lookup applies EMAIL_AUTHENTICATION_AUTO_CONNECT (base.py:209) to silently link matching email to existing user; SPA uses ?process=connect GET links
findings.claim: base.py:204 SOCIALACCOUNT_LOGIN_ON_GET=True makes every /accounts/<provider>/login/ and ?process=connect an unauthenticated GET with no CSRF token (allauth docs flag this as login CSRF); the SPA relies on these GET anchor links (ConnectionsPage.tsx:235 ?process=connect, OnboardingWizard.tsx:127, LoginForm.tsx:113). Combined with SOCIALACCOUNT_EMAIL_AUTHENTICATION_AUTO_CONNECT=True (base.py:209), SOCIALACCOUNT_EMAIL_AUTHENTICATION=True (base.py:212), and every custom provider marked VERIFIED_EMAIL=True (base.py:225-238), a social login whose email matches an existing user is auto-connected with no confirmation. Exploit shapes: forced login (login CSRF) and forced account-linking. The OAuth state param protects the callback so it is not one-click takeover; residual risk is GET initiation + silent auto-connect, sharpest for the custom providers whose VERIFIED_EMAIL guarantee underlies the known OCS/merge findings.

findings.title: OCS email-domain allowlist is open by default and bypassed by no-email logins; .env.example contradicts the coded default
findings.claim: SOCIALACCOUNT_ALLOWED_EMAIL_DOMAINS default restricts only 'commcare' to dimagi.com (providers absent from the dict are unrestricted), but .env.example documents the default as restricting commcare, commcare_connect, AND ocs. config/deploy.yml does not set the env var, so in production OCS (which returns an email via openid) and commcare_connect OAuth accept any email domain; with SOCIALACCOUNT_AUTO_SIGNUP=True any OCS-authenticated user can self-provision a Scout account with auto-resolved tenants and auto-created workspaces. pre_social_login also returns/allows when the login carries no email (allow-by-default-on-missing-data), making provider-asserted unverified emails an auth factor for the three custom providers. Doc and code disagree on a security control.

issues.title: Account-merge correctness (privilege propagation, metadata cascade)
issues.summary: merge_users OR-propagates is_staff/is_superuser from the deleted duplicate (invisible at the y/N prompt -- plausibly already prod state); the verified-email merge gate is subtle; conflict paths cascade-delete the duplicate's TenantMetadata (live + historical migration 0004).

findings.title: merge_users OR-propagates is_staff/is_superuser from the deleted duplicate onto the canonical account, invisible at the confirmation prompt
findings.chain: merge_duplicate_users.py handle() -> merge_users(canonical, duplicate) -> merge.py:235-240 canonical.is_staff/is_superuser |= duplicate flags; _print_plan omits the flag change
findings.claim: apps/users/services/merge.py:235-240 grants the canonical user is_staff/is_superuser if the duplicate had them. Cleaning up a stale createsuperuser dev artifact by merging it into a normal OAuth user silently promotes that user to production superuser (full admin capabilities). The merge_duplicate_users command's printed plan summarizes repoint counts but does not surface the privilege escalation at the y/N prompt. Given the 2026-06 prod merge of exactly such an artifact, this is plausibly already production state.

findings.title: Account merge gate requires a verified EmailAddress the system never produces; local signup bypasses allauth so OAuth-with-same-email can't link
findings.claim: reconcile_existing_user_on_login refuses to auto-merge unless the canonical user has a verified EmailAddress, but ACCOUNT_EMAIL_VERIFICATION='optional' with no production email backend and signup_view creates users via create_user without ever invoking allauth, so no EmailAddress row is created for password accounts and the CommCare/Connect/OCS OAuth flow never creates one either. The verified-email gate is therefore a near-constant False and auto-merge is effectively dead for exactly its target population (password-then-OAuth, or two providers for one human). Duplicate User rows accumulate with TenantMemberships/WorkspaceMemberships split across both rows (two answers to 'what can this user touch'), recoverable only via the operator merge command. The refusal itself is secure-by-design; shipping flows whose accounts can never satisfy the gate is the incoherence. SOCIALACCOUNT_EMAIL_AUTHENTICATION makes provider-asserted emails an auth factor, unverified for the three custom providers.

findings.title: User-merge conflict path cascade-deletes the duplicate's TenantMetadata and can orphan connection wiring
findings.claim: _merge_tenant_memberships deletes the duplicate user's conflicting TenantMembership rows when the canonical already has that tenant; TenantMetadata is a OneToOne on TenantMembership with on_delete=CASCADE, so discovered provider metadata (including team info) riding on the duplicate's membership is destroyed even when the canonical membership carries none — defeating the model's stated purpose of surviving teardown to skip re-discovery. The surviving membership can also be left connection=None when only the duplicate's was wired to a credential. Recoverable via re-discovery on next materialization; rare, transactional.

findings.title: users migration 0004 dedup can cascade-delete TenantMetadata on the discarded duplicate membership (historical, one-time)
findings.chain: users 0004 keeps earliest TenantMembership, deletes later dups -> TenantMetadata.tenant_membership CASCADE OneToOne (workspaces 0001 predates) -> metadata on a later duplicate cascade-deleted
findings.claim: Full RunPython/RunSQL sweep found exactly three data migrations (users 0003/0004/0007; zero RunSQL anywhere). 0003 is trivially safe. 0004 keeps the earliest-created TenantMembership and deletes later duplicates; TenantMetadata.tenant_membership is a CASCADE OneToOne created before this migration (workspaces 0001 2026-03-17 vs users 0004 2026-04-22), so discovery metadata attached to a later duplicate was deleted while the kept membership may have had none. Same earliest-row-over-row-with-data pattern as the known merge-conflict metadata loss, at an earlier site. Already executed; relevant as pattern evidence only.

issues.title: Django admin lockdown + management-command fixes
issues.summary: Admin is an unguarded privileged-write surface: editable state-machine rows re-arm DROP SCHEMA CASCADE, unthrottled login, plaintext tokens, self-escalation; registration inverted (dangerous rows editable, operator models absent); AgentLearningAdmin renders escaped HTML; setup_oauth_apps composes wrong env names; backfill_readonly_roles aborts on first drift.

findings.title: Django admin is an unguarded privileged-write surface: editable state-machine rows can re-arm DROP SCHEMA CASCADE; unthrottled login bypasses allauth/auth rate limiter; plaintext OAuth tokens & client secrets exposed; self-escalation via UserAdmin
findings.chain: config/urls.py:82 admin mounted unconditionally -> TenantSchemaAdmin readonly only id/created_at; operator saves EXPIRED as ACTIVE with stale last_accessed_at -> apps/workspaces/tasks.py:538-544 expire_inactive_schemas defers teardown_schema -> DROP SCHEMA CASCADE; AdminSite.login -> ModelBackend no throttle (contrast auth_views.py:109,150 per-email limiter); base.py:65 allauth.socialaccount auto-registers SocialToken/SocialApp admin -> plaintext tokens+client secrets; users/admin.py is_staff/is_superuser editable -> self-escalation
findings.claim: The Django admin is mounted unconditionally in production (config/urls.py:82, nginx.prod-kamal.conf proxies /admin/) and is a parallel privileged-write/identity surface with no hardening. (1) Dangerous editable state: TenantSchemaAdmin marks only id/created_at readonly, so state/schema_name/last_accessed_at on TenantSchema (and state/result/progress on MaterializationRun, status on TransformationRun) are fully editable. An operator flipping EXPIRED->ACTIVE without touching last_accessed_at re-arms expire_inactive_schemas into teardown_schema -> DROP SCHEMA CASCADE (operator-keyboard incident-b); stale change-form saves clobber live transitions (lost update); schema_name edits desync the physical schema/_ro role; default delete cascade orphans physical schema+role. (2) /admin/login/ uses stock AdminSite->ModelBackend with no rate limit/lockout/2FA, bypassing both allauth and Scout's own 5/300s per-email limiter (which guards only /api/auth/ and is per-process LocMemCache); production.py adds no admin restriction; target superusers exist via the documented createsuperuser path. (3) allauth auto-registers SocialTokenAdmin/SocialAppAdmin, exposing every user's CommCare/Connect/OCS access+refresh tokens and platform OAuth client secrets in plaintext behind the weakest login. (4) UserAdmin exposes is_staff/is_superuser/user_permissions editable, so any staff with users.change_user self-escalates to superuser. Procrastinate's bundled admin (same process) shows the correct readonly/denied pattern.

findings.title: Admin registration inverted: dangerous raw rows fully editable while every model operators need (ThreadJob, WorkspaceViewSchema, memberships, connections) is absent; RecipeAdmin manages vestigial RecipeStep and omits live Recipe.prompt
findings.chain: grep of all @admin.register/admin.site.register in apps/ shows missing operator models + editable TenantSchema.state; recipes/models.py:53 prompt consumed at runner.py:195 but admin.py:39-63 fieldsets omit it; admin.py:11-17,74-90,166-171 build on dead RecipeStep
findings.claim: Workspace, WorkspaceTenant, WorkspaceMembership, WorkspaceViewSchema, ThreadJob, Thread, Tenant, TenantMembership, TenantConnection and TenantMetadata have no admin registration; every recent incident (zombie jobs, stuck Preparing…, EXPIRED resurrection, cascade-FAILED view schemas) required inspecting/repairing exactly these rows via psql or bespoke commands — while TenantSchema.state sits fully editable. The shipped admin surface maximizes operator harm and minimizes operator help; read-only registrations (procrastinate's bundled admin is the in-process template) would invert both. Separately, RecipeAdmin's fieldsets omit the live Recipe.prompt (the only field the runner executes) and are built around the dead RecipeStep model (inline + ModelAdmin + step_count/step_progress), inviting operators to create rows nothing reads; soft-deleted recipes/artifacts are invisible/unrestorable because the soft-delete manager is the default manager.

findings.title: AgentLearningAdmin confidence_badge relies on removed allow_tags; renders escaped literal HTML
findings.chain: knowledge/admin.py:134-144 raw f-string span + allow_tags=True -> Django 5 escapes -> literal HTML text in list column
findings.claim: knowledge/admin.py:134-144 returns a raw f-string span and sets confidence_badge.allow_tags=True; allow_tags was removed in Django 2.0, so on Django 5 the list column renders the literal escaped '<span style=...>' text instead of a colored badge. Purely cosmetic; the adjacent admins use format_html correctly.

findings.title: setup_oauth_apps composes wrong env-var names for Google/GitHub (double _OAUTH_); skip message and docstring name two further spellings, so Google/GitHub can never bootstrap via this command
findings.chain: setup_oauth_apps.py:16-22 env_prefix='GOOGLE_OAUTH' -> :46-47 reads GOOGLE_OAUTH_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID -> always empty -> :50 skip message names GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID; docstring :3-4 a third set; deploy.yml:47-52 real var names
findings.claim: env_prefix is 'GOOGLE_OAUTH'/'GITHUB_OAUTH' and the lookup appends '_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID', so the command reads GOOGLE_OAUTH_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID / GITHUB_OAUTH_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID; the skip message prints '{prefix}_CLIENT_ID not set' (a third spelling) and the docstring promises GOOGLE_OAUTH_* / COMMCARE_CONNECT_OAUTH_* (actual: CONNECT_OAUTH_* per deploy.yml). An operator following the docstring or the error message can never bootstrap Google/GitHub via this command — it silently skips forever. Unused by the current prod deploy (only COMMCARE/CONNECT/OCS vars passed) and recoverable by hand-creating SocialApp in admin, hence LATENT.

findings.title: backfill_readonly_roles aborts on first Django-vs-physical schema drift, leaving later schemas role-less (fail-closed query outage); selects dead MATERIALIZING state; no per-schema error handling, dry-run, or view-role default privileges
findings.chain: backfill_readonly_roles.py:34-36/43-63 loops with no exception handling -> GRANT USAGE on dropped schema raises InvalidSchemaName -> handle() aborts -> remaining ACTIVE schemas never backfilled -> query.py:44 SET ROLE <schema>_ro fails (fail-closed outage)
findings.claim: The SET-ROLE-defense backstop command has no try/except around its per-schema loops: any ACTIVE TenantSchema row whose physical schema is missing (drift producers: MCP teardown_schema, purge_synced_data) makes GRANT USAGE raise InvalidSchemaName and aborts the whole command, so every schema after it never gets its _ro role and all their MCP queries fail at SET ROLE until re-run after manual repair. The command's stated idempotency IS true (pg_roles check + DuplicateObject suppression + idempotent GRANTs + autocommit). Lesser issues: it selects the dead MATERIALIZING state; the view-schema branch grants are point-in-time (no ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES for the view role on tenant schemas) so it cannot repair a workspace between rematerialization and view rebuild; no --dry-run/logging unlike purge_synced_data.

issues.title: Infra/network security: credential separation, subnets, egress, CI role
issues.summary: One DB + one master-superuser credential for both planes; RDS/Redis in public subnets, SSH 0.0.0.0/0, admin internet-exposed; wide-open egress + IMDSv1 (loader SSRF -> instance-role theft); CI deploy role reads every RDS master password in the account. (Some items are prod-stack facts, not in-repo.)

findings.title: One DB, one master-superuser credential: platform and managed/tenant planes co-located, app connects as RDS master
findings.chain: infra/scout-stack.yml:249-271 (single RDS, DBName agent_platform, MasterUsername platform) -> scripts/resolve-database-url.sh:34-37 (DATABASE_URL user=platform=RDS master) -> .kamal/secrets:42-43 (MANAGED_DATABASE_URL=$DATABASE_URL)
findings.claim: The stack provisions exactly one database (agent_platform) with a single master user 'platform'. resolve-database-url.sh emits DATABASE_URL as postgresql://platform:<pw>@endpoint/agent_platform, and .kamal/secrets sets MANAGED_DATABASE_URL=$DATABASE_URL. So the platform plane (Django users/sessions/encrypted TenantConnection credentials/LangGraph checkpoints/procrastinate queue) and the managed/tenant data plane (ws_* schemas, view schemas) live in the SAME database and are both accessed as the RDS master (rds_superuser). The agent SQL path's SET ROLE + SET search_path is the only confinement, running atop a superuser that can SET ROLE back. This is the infra root under the existing transform-as-superuser / SET-ROLE-only-defense / cross-tenant pg_catalog findings: it is one RDS instance, one DB, one superuser credential in production.

findings.title: RDS/Redis in public subnets; broad management surface (SSH 0.0.0.0/0 + SSM); Django admin internet-exposed
findings.chain: infra/scout-stack.yml:241-247 + 275-281 (RDS/Redis subnet groups -> PublicSubnetA/B) with 47-92 (public subnets, MapPublicIpOnLaunch, IGW route), mitigated only by 268 PubliclyAccessible:false + 130/145 SG source restriction; 104-106 SSH 0.0.0.0/0; 171 AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore; frontend/nginx.prod-kamal.conf:90-96 /admin/ proxied
findings.claim: Posture bundle, all verified: (a) RDS and Redis sit in public subnets (DBSubnetGroup/RedisSubnetGroup reference PublicSubnetA/B which have MapPublicIpOnLaunch and an IGW route); they are not internet-reachable today only due to PubliclyAccessible:false plus SG source restriction, so a single SG edit adding a CidrIp rule would directly expose the datastores. (b) Two admin entry paths to the single host: SSH 22 open to 0.0.0.0/0 (key-only) AND SSM Session Manager via AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore. (c) nginx.prod-kamal.conf proxies /admin/ to Django, so https://scout.dimagi.com/admin/ is a public Django admin login surface. Each is standard hardening missing with no functional cost.

findings.title: Wide-open egress + IMDSv1: loader SSRF unbounded, instance-role credential theft reachable
findings.chain: infra/scout-stack.yml:96-119 (EC2SecurityGroup ingress-only, default allow-all egress) -> infra/scout-stack.yml:188-232 (no MetadataOptions -> IMDSv1) -> SSRF reach = internet + VPC + IMDS GET returns scout-ec2-role creds
findings.claim: No SecurityGroupEgress is defined on any of the three security groups, so the AWS default (allow all outbound to 0.0.0.0/0, all ports) applies; and the EC2 instance sets no MetadataOptions, so IMDSv1 (HttpTokens=optional) is reachable by a plain GET. A loader that follows a server-supplied next URL can therefore reach the entire internet for exfil, the VPC-internal RDS/Redis, and http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/scout-ec2-role to steal the instance-role credentials. There is zero network-level compensating control behind the missing app-layer URL validation; TODO.md's unchecked 'loader network egress restriction' is confirmed genuinely absent.

findings.title: CI deploy role over-scoped: account-wide secret prefix wildcards read every RDS master password
findings.chain: infra/scout-stack.yml:438-447 (GetSecretValue Resource includes secret:rds!* and four secret:<PREFIX>_* wildcards) -> infra/scout-stack.yml:448-451 (BatchGetSecretValue on Resource '*')
findings.claim: GitHubDeployRole's secretsmanager:GetSecretValue is scoped by account-wide prefix wildcards, not specific ARNs: rds!* matches every RDS-managed master-password secret in the account (not just scout-db), and SCOUT_*/COMMCARE_*/CONNECT_*/OCS_* match any same-prefixed secret regardless of owner. It also grants BatchGetSecretValue on Resource '*'. Trust is correctly pinned to repo:dimagi-rad/scout:ref:refs/heads/main, but within a CI run the role reads more than it needs.

issues.title: Knowledge / Data Dictionary correctness fixes
issues.summary: TableKnowledge keyed by physical schema name (every refresh orphans annotations; multi-tenant can never match); autosave silently wipes related_tables; import 500s + round-trip loses duplicate-titled entries; learning lifecycle inert while the prompt implies usage. Candidate inputs to content-satellite-redesign.

findings.title: TableKnowledge keyed by physical schema-qualified name: refresh orphans annotations, multi-tenant mismatch, stale names injected into prompts
findings.claim: TableKnowledge.table_name stores qualified_name = f'{schema_name}.{table_name}' from the current physical tenant schema. create_refresh_schema mints a new {base}_r{uuid8} name on every refresh and teardown changes the live name, after which dictionary lookups under the new name miss all annotations (silently vanish from UI), while KnowledgeRetriever keeps injecting stale '### old_schema.table' headings into the system prompt (names the SQL validator rejects and that the agent never queries). In multi-tenant workspaces annotations attach to tenants.first()'s physical schema while the agent queries ws_* {prefix}__{table} views — annotation guidance names a schema the agent shouldn't query. Same stored-physical-reference defect class as the known artifacts-broken-by-rename incident; Learning.applies_to_tables/related_tables are bare-string matched, same family.

findings.title: Data Dictionary annotation autosave silently wipes related_tables (and other list/dict fields) on every edit
findings.chain: TableDetail.tsx:157-166 autosave payload (no related_tables) -> dictionarySlice.ts:245-255 PUT -> apps/workspaces/api/views.py:537-538 related_tables = data.get('related_tables', []); tk.related_tables = related_tables -> views.py:556 tk.save() wipes stored value
findings.claim: TableDetail's 1s-debounced autosave payload omits related_tables (and description), but the backend PUT does data.get('related_tables', []) and unconditionally assigns it, so any admin-curated related_tables annotation (consumed by the agent-context retriever) is destroyed the first time a user types in any annotation field. description survives because its .get() default is the existing value; the list/dict fields are clobber-with-default. Partial-update semantics differ per field with no contract on either side.

findings.title: Knowledge import 500s on malformed input / duplicate titles; export-import round trip silently loses duplicate-titled entries
findings.claim: KnowledgeImportView catches only zipfile.BadZipFile, so parse_frontmatter's text.index ValueError on unterminated frontmatter, yaml.YAMLError, UnicodeDecodeError on non-UTF8 members, and MultipleObjectsReturned from update_or_create on (workspace,title) (KnowledgeEntry has no uniqueness constraint and create allows duplicate titles) all 500 mid-import, leaving a partial non-atomic import. On export, duplicate titles produce duplicate zip filenames; re-import keeps only the last — silent entry loss on a round trip. Export/import also covers KnowledgeEntry only; TableKnowledge annotations and AgentLearning have no portability path.

findings.title: Learning lifecycle is inert: confidence never auto-adjusts, times_applied effectively never increments, but the prompt implies real usage
findings.claim: The advertised feedback loop doesn't exist: increase/decrease_confidence are callable only from Django admin bulk actions (the model help_text claims auto-adjust on confirm/contradict); times_applied increments only when the agent re-saves a byte-identical (iexact) description — essentially never for LLM prose — yet the retriever renders '(applied N times)' implying real usage. Drifted learnings never retire. save_learning's only validation compares applies_to_tables against the dead Workspace.data_dictionary field (always empty), so it is permanently skipped.

issues.title: Provider loader data-quality + upstream-contract fixes
issues.summary: Unguarded inbound payloads (>255 names, missing keys, NUMERIC overflow); dead/wrong columns (raw_visits.images, participant_platform, unstable message_id, Connect count always None); next-URL trust (Connect plaintext http://); resumability split across two registries; offset-skip (Forms v0.5 only); denominator accuracy >10k.

findings.title: Inbound provider payload sizes/shapes unguarded: long canonical_name DataErrors swallowed, missing/empty natural keys collapse or crash writers, NUMERIC out-of-range, money precision
findings.claim: Tenant.canonical_name (CharField 255) and User.email are filled directly from Connect/OCS/CommCare API responses with no bound; a >255-char name raises DataError mid-resolution swallowed as a login warning (user silently sees no opportunities). Writer boundary trusts every provider row has a non-null non-empty natural key: Connect visits maps visit_id BIGINT PK from raw.get('id') -> None -> NotNullViolation fails the page (same class as 2587158); many writers (Connect users, OCS sessions/experiments/participants/messages, CommCare cases/forms) default TEXT PKs to '' and ON CONFLICT, so two id-less rows silently collapse to one (wrong count, no error); OCS messages silently skips id-less sessions — three inconsistent behaviors for one condition, none logged. Connect money fields write into NUMERIC(14,2) with no range check (>=1e12 or minor-units raises NumericValueOutOfRange failing the page). Connect cursor _max_id depends on a payload 'id' field not persisted/asserted (silent resume disable / stale-watermark duplication on drift).

findings.title: raw_visits.images is always empty: upstream only emits images with ?images=true, which Scout never sends, yet the writer creates and upserts an images JSONB column
findings.chain: connect_visits.py:76-78 _paginate_export_pages('user_visits/') no params -> upstream UserVisitDataView.get_serializer_class: images only when query_params['images']=='true' -> connect_visits.py:39-41,65 defaults to [] -> materializer ~L1445 images JSONB always []
findings.claim: Verified verbatim: UserVisitDataView selects UserVisitDataWithImagesSerializer only when request has images=true; the default serializer omits the 'images' key entirely. ConnectVisitLoader passes no params, so raw.get('images') or [] is always []. Yet the writer creates an images JSONB column and upserts it (images=EXCLUDED.images, materializer ~L1273/L1445/L1483) — an advertised, perpetually-empty column visible to the agent and analysts. Either request images=true (payload-size tradeoff) or drop the column.

findings.title: raw_sessions.participant_platform always '' (OCS nested participant serializer has no platform field); sessions->participants join on bare identifier conflates same-identifier participants across platforms
findings.chain: ocs_sessions.py:39-57 _map_session participant.get('platform') -> upstream ParticipantSerializer fields=['identifier','remote_id'] -> participant_platform always '' in raw_sessions; ocs_sync.yml join on bare identifier conflates across platforms
findings.claim: Verified verbatim: ParticipantSerializer (nested in ExperimentSessionSerializer) has fields=['identifier','remote_id'] only. Scout's _map_session reads participant.get('platform') — a key the provider never sends — so participant_platform is empty for every row, while the session-level 'platform' field that does exist is ignored. Additionally, OCS participants are unique per (team, platform, identifier) (get_or_create in ExperimentSessionCreateSerializer), so the ocs_sync.yml sessions->participants join on bare identifier can conflate same-identifier participants across platforms.

findings.title: OCS session detail interleaves synthetic unsaved 'summary' messages; Scout's positional message_id is unstable across syncs (raises the resumability-registry finding's blast radius)
findings.chain: ocs_messages.py:47-50 detail fetch -> upstream get_messages -> chat/models.py:73-81 message_iterator(with_summaries=True) yields synthetic summaries -> ocs_messages.py:64-66 message_id=f'{session_id}:{index}' positional -> materializer L1368 ON CONFLICT (message_id)
findings.claim: Verified verbatim upstream: ExperimentSessionSerializer.get_messages calls chat.message_iterator() whose default with_summaries=True yields each message PLUS a synthetic, unsaved summary message when one exists (apps/chat/models.py:73-81). Scout builds message_id = f'{session_id}:{index}' from enumeration order. Consequences: (a) raw_messages overcounts — machine-generated summaries are indistinguishable from real traffic except via metadata.compression_marker; (b) message_index/message_id shift whenever OCS later compresses history and a summary appears mid-array, so ids are not stable across syncs even for append-only chats. Safe today only because ocs:messages does full DROP/CREATE/INSERT; if the unsafe default-True resumability registry (already-reported finding) ever treats it as resumable, positional drift silently corrupts the table via ON CONFLICT (message_id) DO UPDATE. Content-hash or (session, created_at, role) ids would remove the hazard.

findings.title: Connect v2 export responses never contain 'count': progress totals always None and loader docstrings describe a nonexistent field
findings.chain: upstream IdKeysetPagination.get_paginated_response returns {'next','results'} only -> connect_base.py:165-167 reads payload.get('count') (always None) -> page_total threaded to rows_total -> indeterminate progress; docstring claims a count field that never exists
findings.claim: Verified verbatim: IdKeysetPagination.get_paginated_response returns only {'next', 'results'} — no count, ever. Scout's connect_base.py reads payload.get('count') and documents 'total_count is the count field from the first response' (connect_base.py:165-167, 225-229); every Connect loader threads page_total to the materializer's rows_total. All Connect materialization progress is therefore permanently indeterminate (honest, but undocumented as such), and the docstring is a comment-as-claim failure. Contrast: OCS upstream added a first-page 'count' explicitly citing Scout as the consumer; nobody requested the same from Connect.

findings.title: Case v2 matching_records accuracy above 10k unverified (possible ES total-hits cap on the progress denominator)
findings.chain: get_list.py matching_records = es_result.total -> es_query.py:588-590 returns raw['hits']['total'] (track_total_hits unverified) -> commcare_cases.py page_total -> materializer L868-872 progress denominator only
findings.claim: get_list.py sets matching_records = es_result.total and corehq/apps/es/es_query.py:588-590 returns raw['hits']['total'] as-is; whether HQ's ES client requests exact totals (track_total_hits) or returns the ES-default 10,000-capped value could not be traced (no track_total_hits hits in corehq/apps/es/{client,es_query,utils}.py). Scout impact is bounded: page_total only feeds the progress denominator (materializer L868-872), so worst case is a wrong progress bar on >10k-case domains.

findings.title: CommCare offset pagination under live writes can silently skip records
findings.claim: The CommCare forms loader walks tastypie limit/offset pages with no explicit ordering; submissions arriving mid-walk shift offsets — duplicates are absorbed by ON CONFLICT (but inflate the recorded row count via total += len(page)), while skips are silent and permanent until the next full reload. Connect's keyset design avoids this; CommCare never got an equivalent watermark.

findings.title: Known finding correction: Case API v2 cannot silently skip under live writes (keyset, dups-at-end by design); skip risk is Forms v0.5 only, narrower than claimed
findings.chain: commcare_cases.py:38-69 follows v2 next -> upstream get_list.py keyset sort('@indexed_on').sort('doc_id') -> docs: updated cases reappear at end -> materializer L1088 ON CONFLICT (case_id) absorbs; forms: commcare_forms.py:38-71 offset walk -> v0_4.py:157 order_by('-received_on') tastypie limit/offset
findings.claim: The reported finding 'CommCare offset pagination under live writes can silently skip records' is REFUTED for the cases loader: Case API v2 is keyset pagination on ('@indexed_on','doc_id') and the docs guarantee a complete oldest-to-newest set where concurrently-updated cases REAPPEAR at the end — duplicates Scout's ON CONFLICT (case_id) absorbs. For Forms v0.5 the claim stands but with corrected mechanism: it is tastypie offset pagination over ES with default ordering '-received_on' DESC (v0_4.py:157), so live inserts shift rows DOWN causing duplicates (absorbed by ON CONFLICT (form_id)), and silent skips occur only when documents leave the result set mid-walk (form archiving/deletion), not from ordinary submission traffic. Severity should be lowered for cases and the forms mechanism re-stated.

findings.title: Loaders follow server-supplied next URLs anywhere with session-pinned credentials (no host/scheme validation)
findings.claim: connect_base and ocs_base take the next request URL from the response body; auth lives in session headers which requests sends to whatever host/scheme that URL names (header stripping applies only to redirects, not body-supplied URLs), and there is no host/scheme validation. Production Connect already returns scheme-downgraded http:// next URLs (acknowledged in code). A compromised/misbehaving provider could redirect credentialed requests to an arbitrary host. Related unchecked TODO.md item: loader/export network egress is unrestricted.

findings.title: Next-URL trust verified per provider: Case v2 next is settings-derived absolute (safe today); Forms v0.5 relative (handled); Connect next is proxy-header-derived and emitted plaintext http:// in prod (#1109), sending Bearer over plaintext first hop
findings.chain: connect_base.py:186-197 session.get(payload['next']) with Authorization header -> upstream pagination.py get_next_link() request.build_absolute_uri -> #1109: gunicorn stripped X-Forwarded-Proto -> http:// next URL -> plaintext request with Bearer, then 301 to https
findings.claim: F7-class verification refining the 'follow next URLs anywhere' finding. (1) Case API v2 'next' is built via corehq reverse(absolute=True) -> get_url_base() = settings.DEFAULT_PROTOCOL + settings.BASE_ADDRESS — always absolute, host from server config; the relative-next trigger is REFUTED for v2, so commcare_cases.py's missing _resolve_next_url is harmless today. (2) Forms v0.5 (tastypie) emits path-relative next links — Scout's _resolve_next_url handles correctly. (3) Connect's IdKeysetPagination.get_next_link uses request.build_absolute_uri — scheme/host from proxy headers; merged PR dimagi/commcare-connect#1109 confirms production emitted http:// next URLs (gunicorn --forwarded-allow-ips default stripped X-Forwarded-Proto), during which Scout's session.get(next) sent the OAuth Bearer over plaintext http for the first hop before following the 301. A Scout-side scheme/host pin would close the recurrence class.

findings.title: Resumability truth lives in two contradictory registries (_RESUMABLE_CONNECT_SOURCES vs YAML) with an unsafe default-True; non-resumable Connect writers still commit per page
findings.claim: Resumability is decided in run_pipeline from source.resumable AND the hardcoded set _RESUMABLE_CONNECT_SOURCES, which still lists the five sources (completed_works, payments, invoices, assessments, completed_modules) that commit 2587158 made non-resumable in connect_sync.yml (resumable:false). YAML currently wins via the AND, but pipeline_registry defaults resumable=True, so a new Connect source without an explicit YAML flag gets cursor-resume against a plain-INSERT (no ON CONFLICT) writer, silently reintroducing the f26c1a0 row-duplication class.
